title: Select Cells in Grid With Maximum Score
performanceTarget: top 1% execution time and overall performance
note: Pareto-optimal challenge: the single C++ matching-greedy solution is also the speed extreme and the memory extreme. Per the repo standard (no coincident files), only solution.cpp is shipped; there is no separate solution-runtime or solution-memory file.

variants.languageReason: The workload is fixed-domain integer matching over at most 100 values and 10 rows. C++ gives native branch-heavy performance, compact fixed arrays, direct bit intrinsics, and the standard LeetCode vector matrix signature without GC or interpreter overhead.

languageAlternatives.assessment: Selected for native speed, fixed arrays, direct bit operations, and ergonomic LeetCode integration.
Considered for the smallest runtime baseline; it does not improve the algorithmic memory profile and would mainly trade readability and platform ergonomics for tiny baseline constants.
Considered for native performance and memory safety; nested Vec access and borrow-management overhead add ceremony without improving the tiny fixed-state workload.
Considered for compiled loops and simple slices; runtime, bounds checks, and GC baseline are less attractive than C++ fixed arrays here.
Considered for strong JIT performance; object-array layout and managed runtime overhead are unnecessary for 101 masks and 10 row assignments.
Considered because the constraints are small; interpreter/VM overhead loses to native code for top-percentile runtime.

variants.approachJustification: Model distinct values as weighted elements that can be matched to rows where they appear. Matchable value sets are a transversal matroid, so descending-value greedy is optimal; each keep/reject decision is tested by one augmenting path in the current row matching.

reasoningSummary: Collapse duplicate cells into value-to-row bitmasks. The chosen values must be matchable injectively to rows, which is the independent-set family of a transversal matroid. Greedy by descending value, backed by augmenting paths to maintain a matching, returns the maximum-weight feasible value set.
